title: Production possibility curves
summary:
## The big idea The **production possibility curve (PPC)** — also called the production possibility frontier — is the simplest and most useful diagram in the whole course. On one picture it shows scarcity, choice, opportunity cost, efficiency, unemployment and growth. It shows the **maximum combinations of two goods** an economy can produce when all its resources are **fully and efficiently used**, with technology fixed.
**What each part means:** the axes show two types of good (here **capital goods** vs **consumer goods**). The **red curve (PPC1)** is the frontier — the maximum output when all resources are fully and efficiently used. **Point A** is *on* the curve = efficient and fully employed. **Point B** is *inside* = resources wasted or unemployed. **Point C** is *outside* = currently unattainable. The **dashed curve (PPC2)** shows **economic growth** — more or better resources shift the whole frontier outward, making C reachable.
## On, inside and outside the curve A point **on the curve** (A) means the economy is **productively efficient** and fully employing its resources — it is doing as well as it currently can. A point **inside the curve** (B) means resources are **unemployed or used inefficiently**, as in a recession. A point **outside the curve** (C) is currently **unattainable** — the economy simply does not have the resources to reach it yet. ## Opportunity cost on the PPC Moving along the curve means producing **more of one good and less of the other** — and that sacrifice *is* the opportunity cost, shown geometrically. The **shape** of the curve reveals how opportunity cost behaves. A **straight-line** PPC means **constant** opportunity cost (resources are equally good at both jobs). The usual **curved (concave to the origin)** shape means **increasing** opportunity cost: because resources are *not* equally suited to both goods, the more you specialise in one good the larger the amount of the other you must give up. ## Shifts of the PPC — economic growth When the whole curve shifts **outward** (PPC1 → PPC2), the economy can produce more of *both* goods — this is **economic growth**. It happens when there is an increase in the **quantity** of resources (a bigger workforce, more capital from investment, new resource discoveries) or in their **quality** (education and training, better technology). A fall in resources — war, natural disaster, mass emigration — shifts the curve **inward**. ## The capital-vs-consumer-goods trade-off A powerful use of the PPC is the choice between making **capital goods** or **consumer goods** today. Producing more capital goods now means sacrificing current consumption, but capital raises future productive capacity — so the economy enjoys a **bigger outward shift** later. Choosing mostly consumer goods raises living standards now but limits future growth. This is the trade-off between the present and the future. ## Actual vs potential growth Finally, the PPC clarifies a key macro distinction. **Potential growth** is an *outward shift* of the curve (capacity rises). **Actual growth** is moving from a point *inside* the curve *towards* it, by using up spare capacity. You will meet this again as shifts of LRAS versus closing an output gap.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **PPC** = max combinations of two goods with resources fully & efficiently used, technology fixed. - **On** curve = efficient/full employment; **inside** = unemployment/inefficiency; **outside** = unattainable. - Moving along the curve shows **opportunity cost**; **concave** shape = **increasing** opportunity cost; straight line = constant. - **Outward shift** = economic growth (more/better resources or technology); inward shift = fall in resources. - **Capital vs consumer goods**: more capital today → bigger future outward shift. - **Potential growth** = outward shift; **actual growth** = moving from inside towards the curve.
revisionNotes:
# Production possibility curve — quick revision

**What it shows**
- Max output combinations of **two goods** with resources fully & efficiently used (tech fixed).

**Points**
- **On** the curve = productively efficient, fully employed.
- **Inside** = unemployment / inefficiency (e.g. recession).
- **Outside** = currently unattainable.

**Opportunity cost & shape**
- Moving along the curve = giving up one good for the other (opportunity cost).
- **Concave (bowed out)** = increasing opportunity cost (resources not equally suited); **straight line** = constant.

**Shifts**
- **Outward** = economic growth (more/better resources, technology, investment).
- **Inward** = fewer resources (war, disaster, emigration).

**Trade-offs & growth**
- More **capital goods** now → bigger future outward shift (vs consumer goods now).
- **Potential growth** = outward shift; **actual growth** = moving from inside toward the curve.
questions:
# Practice questions — Production possibility curves **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A point *inside* a country's PPC indicates: - A — economic growth - B — productive efficiency - C — unemployed or inefficiently used resources - D — an unattainable output
▸ Show answer

C. Inside the curve, some resources are idle or misused, so the economy could produce more.

**2.** A PPC that is concave (bowed outward from the origin) shows: - A — constant opportunity cost - B — increasing opportunity cost - C — zero opportunity cost - D — falling output
▸ Show answer

B. The concave shape reflects increasing opportunity cost because resources are not equally suited to both goods.

**3.** An outward shift of the entire PPC is caused by: - A — a fall in unemployment - B — moving resources between the two goods - C — an increase in the quantity or quality of resources - D — a rise in the price level
▸ Show answer

C. More/better resources or technology raise capacity (economic growth). Reducing unemployment is a move *towards* the curve, not a shift.

**4.** An economy choosing to produce more capital goods and fewer consumer goods today is most likely to experience: - A — a smaller PPC in the future - B — a larger outward shift of the PPC in the future - C — no change in future capacity - D — immediate higher living standards
▸ Show answer

B. Capital goods raise future productive capacity, shifting the PPC further out later (at the cost of consumption now).

## Paper 2 style — data response > After a severe drought and mass emigration of skilled workers, Country Y's output of both food and manufactured goods fell sharply, and factories stood idle. **(a)** Define ‘production possibility curve’. **[2]** **(b)** Using a PPC diagram, explain the effect of the mass emigration on Country Y. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse how idle factories would be shown on a PPC. **[6]** **(d)** ‘Country Y should devote more resources to capital goods than to consumer goods.’ Assess this view. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A curve showing the maximum combinations of two goods producible with resources fully and efficiently used. [1+1]

(b) Diagram: emigration reduces resources → the whole PPC shifts inward. Explain in words + labelled diagram.

(c) Idle factories = unemployed resources → the economy operates at a point inside the PPC, below its frontier.

(d) For: capital raises future capacity/growth. Against: sacrifices current consumption/living standards, uncertain returns, may need consumer goods now after a crisis. Judgement.

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how a PPC illustrates opportunity cost and economic growth. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether an outward shift of the PPC always improves living standards. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Opportunity cost = the sacrifice when moving along the curve; growth = an outward shift from more/better resources. Use a diagram.

(b) Growth raises potential output/incomes, but may bring inequality, pollution, resource depletion, or be in capital goods not consumer goods. ‘Always’ is too strong — judgement.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the difference between a movement along a PPC and a shift of the PPC. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the view that a country in a deep recession should focus on reaching its existing PPC rather than shifting it outward. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Movement = reallocating resources between the two goods (opportunity cost); shift = a change in total capacity. Diagram helps.

(b) In recession there is spare capacity (inside the PPC), so demand-side measures to reach the frontier (actual growth) may matter most short term; but long-run supply-side policy (outward shift) matters too. Judgement on time frame.

keyTerms.definition: A curve showing the maximum combinations of two goods producible with resources fully and efficiently used.
Producing on the PPC, where more of one good requires less of another.
The rising sacrifice of one good as more of another is produced, giving the PPC its concave shape.
An increase in productive capacity, shown by an outward shift of the PPC.
An outward shift of the PPC from more/better resources or technology.
A move from inside the PPC towards it as spare capacity is used.

tips: Label axes with **two specific goods** and mark points **inside, on and outside** the curve.
Explain the **concave shape** using *increasing opportunity cost* — a common higher-mark point.
Use the **capital vs consumer goods** trade-off to discuss investment and future growth.
Link a point **inside** the PPC to **unemployment / a negative output gap**.
diagrams: **PPC (see figure):** two goods on the axes, concave curve; mark A (on = efficient), B (inside = unemployment), C (outside = unattainable) and a dashed outward shift for growth.
**Opportunity cost:** show a movement from one point on the curve to another with arrows (more of one good, less of the other).
